title: Types of Glasses: A Complete Guide to Lenses, Frames, and Styles
description: Every type of glasses explained — from single vision to progressives, aviators to cat-eye. Find the right lenses and frames for your needs and style.
tldr: Glasses fall into categories by lens type (single vision, bifocal, progressive, reading) and frame style (full-rim, semi-rimless, rimless, aviator, cat-eye, round, square, rectangle). Your prescription, lifestyle, and face shape determine the best combination.

definition.text: Glasses (eyeglasses) are optical devices worn on the face to correct refractive errors like nearsightedness, farsightedness, and astigmatism. They are categorized by lens type (how they correct vision) and frame style (their shape and construction).

sections.content:
The lens is the functional part of your glasses — it determines what vision problems they correct and how. Here are the main types:

Single Vision Lenses

The most common type. The entire lens has one prescription power, correcting for either distance vision (nearsightedness/myopia) or near vision (farsightedness/hyperopia). If you're under 40 and need glasses, this is almost certainly what you'll get. Same-day single vision lenses are available at many optical shops.

Bifocal Lenses

Two distinct zones in one lens — distance vision on top, reading on the bottom, with a visible line separating them. Invented by Benjamin Franklin. Still used today, though many people prefer the smoother transition of progressives.

Progressive Lenses

Like bifocals but without the visible line. They provide a gradual transition from distance (top) to intermediate/computer (middle) to reading (bottom). There's an adjustment period of 1–2 weeks, but most people prefer them to bifocals. Your optometrist can tell you if progressives are right for your prescription.

Reading Glasses

Single vision lenses designed specifically for close-up work. Available over-the-counter in standard magnification strengths (+1.00 to +3.50) or as custom prescription readers from your optician.

Computer/Office Lenses

Optimized for intermediate distances (roughly 50cm to 2m) — the range where screens, keyboards, and office work happen. A good option if you spend most of your day at a desk and find progressives too limited in the intermediate zone.

sections.h2: Types of Glasses by Frame Style
sections.content:
Full-Rim Frames

The lens is completely surrounded by frame material. The sturdiest and most versatile option, available in every shape and material imaginable. Best for: everyone, especially active wearers and anyone with a strong prescription (thicker lenses are better hidden in full rims).

Semi-Rimless (Half-Rim) Frames

Frame material runs along the top of the lens only. The bottom edge is held by a thin wire or nylon cord. Clean, professional look. Best for: business settings, lighter prescriptions.

Rimless Frames

No frame around the lens at all — just the lenses, bridge, and temples. The most lightweight and invisible option. Fragile, so not ideal for kids or rough handling. Best for: minimalists, people who want glasses that disappear.

The best way to evaluate frame styles is to try them on in person. Photos and online try-on tools give a rough idea, but they can't replicate how a frame feels on your face — the weight distribution, the temple pressure, and how the nose bridge sits. These comfort factors matter more than aesthetics for glasses you'll wear 12+ hours a day.

Charm Optical in Edmonton carries over 500 frames from brands including Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, Prada, and Dolce & Gabbana. Their opticians help you narrow down the options based on your face shape, prescription requirements (especially important for progressives), and personal style preferences. With same-day service for many prescriptions, you can walk in and walk out with new glasses the same day.

ShapeDescriptionBest Face Shape
RoundCircular lenses, John Lennon styleSquare, angular faces
SquareAngular, geometric, equal proportionsRound, oval faces
RectangleWider than tall, classicRound, oval faces
AviatorTeardrop shape, metalSquare, oblong faces
Cat-EyeUpswept outer cornersRound, square, heart faces
BrowlineBold upper frame, thin lowerOval, heart faces
OvalSoft, slightly elongatedSquare, angular faces
WayfarerTrapezoidal, slightly tiltedMost face shapes

Not sure which shape works for you? The simplest rule: contrast your face shape. Round face? Try angular frames. Angular face? Try round or oval frames. But rules are meant to be broken — if you love a style, try it on. Charm Optical's team in Edmonton can help you experiment and find what clicks.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Safety Glasses

Impact-resistant lenses (polycarbonate or Trivex) with frames that meet CSA Z94.3 standards in Canada. Required in many workplaces. Can be made with your prescription.

Sports Glasses

Wrap-around frames with shatterproof lenses, designed to stay put during activity. Oakley is the gold standard here, with prescription-compatible sport frames.

Blue Light Glasses

Lenses with a coating or tint that filters a portion of blue light from screens and LED lighting. Available as prescription or non-prescription.

Sunglasses (Prescription)

UV-protective lenses with your prescription built in. Essential for driving and outdoor activities. Polarized options reduce glare from water, snow, and roads — particularly useful in Alberta's bright winters. Brands like Ray-Ban and Maui Jim offer excellent prescription sunglass options.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. sections.h2: Frame Materials: What Your Glasses Are Made Of
sections.content:
• Acetate — Plant-based plastic. Rich colours, comfortable, hypoallergenic. The most popular frame material worldwide. Slightly heavier than metal.
  • Metal (Titanium, Stainless Steel) — Lightweight, thin, durable. Titanium is the premium choice — strong, corrosion-resistant, and feather-light.
  • TR-90 (Nylon-based) — Extremely lightweight and flexible. Popular for sport and everyday frames. Memory material that returns to shape.
  • Wood/Bamboo — Eco-friendly, unique look. Less durable and harder to adjust than traditional materials.
  • Mixed — Metal front with acetate temples, or vice versa. Combines the benefits of both materials.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

sections.h2: How to Choose the Right Type of Glasses
sections.content:
Start with your prescription — that determines your lens type. Then consider your lifestyle, face shape, and personal style for the frame. Here's a simple decision flow:

  1. Get a current prescription. Book an eye exam if yours is more than two years old.
  2. Determine your lens type. Under 40 with one issue? Single vision. Over 40 and struggling with both distance and reading? Progressive or bifocal.
  3. Pick your frame style. Consider face shape, lifestyle (active vs. office), and how much you want your glasses to stand out.
  4. Choose your material. Acetate for style variety, metal for lightweight minimalism, TR-90 for durability.
  5. Add coatings. Anti-reflective is almost always worth it. Blue light filtering and photochromic (transitions) are useful add-ons depending on your needs.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter — no upfront costs and no claim forms to submit afterwards.

Whether you're buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the entire process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: What are the 3 main types of prescription glasses?
faqs.a: The three main types are single vision (one focal power for distance or near), bifocal (two focal zones with a visible line), and progressive (multiple focal zones with a seamless gradient). Single vision is the most common, while progressives are the most popular multifocal option.

faqs.q: How do I know what type of glasses I need?
faqs.a: Your eye exam prescription tells you. A prescription with only 'sphere' values typically means single vision. If your optometrist includes an 'ADD' value, you'll need multifocal lenses (progressive or bifocal). Book an eye exam at Charm Optical in Edmonton to find out.

faqs.q: What's the difference between progressive and bifocal glasses?
faqs.a: Bifocals have two distinct zones separated by a visible line. Progressives provide the same distance-to-near correction but with a smooth, gradual transition and no visible line. Progressives look more natural and include an intermediate (computer) zone that bifocals lack.

faqs.q: Can one pair of glasses do everything?
faqs.a: Progressive lenses come closest to an all-in-one solution, covering distance, intermediate, and near vision. However, many people benefit from a dedicated computer pair for long screen sessions or prescription sunglasses for driving.

faqs.q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
faqs.a: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my lenses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.
